did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network (MLP NN), a type of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with Back Propagation learning, to classify dermatoscopic images for melanoma skin cancer detection. First-order feature extraction (contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, smoothness) was performed on grayscale images, and the ANN was trained and tested to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions, achieving an overall accuracy of 83.86%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of first-order feature extraction methods and a Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network to classify dermatoscopic images for melanoma detection. It includes specific methodology, dataset details (23 images), and quantitative results (accuracy rates), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: First-order Feature Extraction Methods for Image Texture and Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection
brief_summary: This study aimed to facilitate early melanoma skin cancer detection by applying first-order texture feature extraction methods to dermatoscopic images. The researchers utilized six parameters—contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, and smoothness—extracted from grayscale images processed via a Possibility Distribution Algorithm and Otsu thresholding. A Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network (MLP NN) with Back Propagation was employed to classify the extracted features into melanoma and non-melanoma categories. The dataset consisted of 23 dermatoscopic images, comprising 15 melanoma and 8 non-melanoma lesions, which were divided into four training and testing sets. The system achieved an overall accuracy of 83.86%, with individual test set accuracies ranging from 81.81% to 85.71%. The authors concluded that while the method is viable, larger and more varied datasets are needed to improve accuracy beyond the current levels.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two diagnostic classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 15 melanoma images.
The comparator class in the binary classification task, representing benign or non-cancerous lesions. The dataset contained 8 non-melanoma images.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network (MLP NN) to classify dermatoscopic images into two discrete classes: melanoma and non-melanoma. The system outputs a decision of whether the input image is melanoma skin cancer or non-melanoma skin cancer. Accuracy metrics (81.81% to 85.71%, overall 83.86%) are reported for this binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermatoscopic Image Collection
datasets_used.description: A private collection of 23 dermatoscopic color images used for melanoma skin cancer detection. The images were converted to grayscale and processed using a Possibility Distribution Algorithm and Otsu thresholding. The dataset consists of 15 melanoma images (labeled melanoma-01.JPG to melanoma-15.JPG) and 8 non-melanoma images (labeled not_01.JPG to not_08.JPG).

use.training: The entire dataset of 23 images was used for training the Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network (MLP NN) via four different training/testing splits (sets I-IV) where the proportion of training images varied from 50% to 80% of the total dataset.
use.testing: The same 23 images were used for testing in four separate sets (Set I: 50% train/50% test, Set II: 60% train/40% test, Set III: 70% train/30% test, Set IV: 80% train/20% test) to evaluate classification accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are reported solely as classification accuracy percentages.

Custom Dermatoscopic Image Collection (2.notes: The lowest accuracy was 81.81% and the highest was 85.71%. The overall average accuracy was 83.86%. The authors concluded that the accuracy level is far from expected and recommended using larger and more varied datasets for future research.

architecture_details.input_features: 6 first-order texture features: contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, and smoothness
architecture_details.layers: Not explicitly reported (standard MLP structure implied)
architecture_details.heads: Binary classification output (melanoma vs. not melanoma)

hyperparameters.fuzzy_possibility_parameters: alpha, beta1, gamma, beta2, max (used in preprocessing, not network weights)

training_details.training_schedule: Iterations continued until certain minimum error criteria were met

preprocessing_techniques: Image quality improvement using Possibility Distribution Algorithm (fuzzy logic approach with parameters alpha, beta1, gamma, beta2, and max)
Image segmentation using Otsu Thresholding (global and local thresholding to separate object from background)
First-order feature extraction (contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, and smoothness) based on histogram analysis
conclusions: The proposed system using first-order feature extraction (contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, and smoothness) combined with a Multilayer Perceptron Neural Network (MLP NN) is viable for classifying dermatoscopic images into melanoma and non-melanoma categories.
The method achieved an overall accuracy of 83.86%, with individual test set accuracies ranging from 81.81% to 85.71% across four different training/testing splits.
The authors concluded that the achieved accuracy level is far from expected and acknowledged that the current dataset size (23 images) is insufficient for optimal performance.
The study recommends using larger and more varied datasets in future research to improve the accuracy of melanoma skin cancer detection systems.
important_remarks: The dataset is extremely small (n=23 images: 15 melanoma, 8 non-melanoma), which severely limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with nearly twice as many melanoma images (15) as non-melanoma images (8).
The source of the images is not specified, raising concerns about potential selection bias and lack of external validation.
The study acknowledges that the achieved accuracy (83.86%) is 'far from expected' and explicitly recommends using larger and more varied datasets for future research.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; results are presented solely as classification accuracy percentages.

The preprocessing pipeline includes a custom 'Possibility Distribution Algorithm' using fuzzy logic parameters (alpha, beta1, gamma, beta2, max) which may introduce arbitrary tuning without sensitivity analysis.

The system relies on first-order texture features (contrast, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, mean, smoothness) extracted from grayscale images, which may miss critical color and higher-order structural information relevant to melanoma diagnosis.

The training and testing splits are not independent in a standard cross-validation sense; the same 23 images are reused across four different split configurations, potentially inflating performance estimates due to data leakage or overfitting to the small pool.
The paper is a conference proceeding (Journal of Physics: Conference Series), which typically undergoes less rigorous peer review than high-impact clinical journals.
